expeditions.title: Algebraic Foundations
expeditions.funFact: The word 'algebra' comes from the title of a 9th-century book by Persian mathematician al-Khwārizmī: 'Al-Kitāb al-mukhtaṣar fī ḥisāb al-jabr wal-muqābala' — the word al-jabr became algebra.
expeditions.jobConnection: Every statistical formula you'll encounter is built on these foundations. Understanding algebra deeply means you can read and manipulate formulas instead of treating them as magic spells.

units.title: Variables, Expressions & Equations
units.description: The building blocks of all mathematical thinking. We're not just reviewing algebra — we're rebuilding your mental model of what math is.
objectives: What variables actually are (and why they're powerful)
The difference between expressions and equations
How to read and manipulate equations with confidence
How all of this shows up in your analytics work
units.orientation: A quick orientation. This unit might feel basic — and parts of it will be. But resist the urge to skim. We're not just reviewing algebra. We're rebuilding your mental model of what math is, so that when things get hard later, you have solid ground to stand on. The single biggest difference between people who 'get' advanced math and those who don't? The second group memorized procedures. The first group understood what the symbols mean. We're going for the second group.

sections.title: What is a Variable?
sections.content:
A variable is simply a name we give to a quantity we don't know yet, or a quantity that can change. That's it. No mystery.

When you write `revenue = price × units_sold`, price and units_sold are variables. They vary. Revenue changes depending on what values they hold. You do this kind of thinking every day — math just gives it a formal notation.

The classic notation uses single letters: x, y, n, p, μ, σ. This is just convention — shorthand. When statisticians write μ (mu), they mean 'the true average of a population.' When they write p, they often mean 'a probability.' The letters are just agreed-upon nicknames.

The key insight: A variable is a placeholder for a value. Once you give it a value, the placeholder becomes concrete.
sections.keyInsight: A variable is a placeholder for a value. Once you give it a value, the placeholder becomes concrete.

sections.title: Expressions vs. Equations
sections.content:
These two words get used interchangeably in conversation. In math, they mean different things.

An expression is a mathematical phrase — it has value, but no claim about equality. `3x + 7` says: 'take some number x, multiply it by 3, then add 7.' It doesn't make a claim. It's just a calculation waiting for a value of x.

An equation is a statement that two things are equal. It always has an = sign. `3x + 7 = 22` means: 'there exists a value of x such that 3x + 7 equals 22.' The equation is either true or false for any given x. Our job is to find the x that makes it true.

Think of it this way: An expression is like a recipe. An equation is like a recipe where someone tells you the dish came out to exactly 600 calories — and you have to figure out how much butter they used.
sections.keyInsight: An expression describes a calculation. An equation makes a claim — that two expressions are equal.

sections.title: The Balance Model
sections.content:
Here's the most important way to think about equations: An equation is a perfectly balanced scale. The = sign is the fulcrum. Both sides weigh exactly the same.

Your only rule is: whatever you do to one side, you must do to the other.

This isn't a trick or a procedure to memorize. It's just logic. If two things are equal and you do the same thing to both of them, they're still equal.

Example: `3x + 7 = 22`

Step 1: Subtract 7 from both sides → `3x = 15`
Step 2: Divide both sides by 3 → `x = 5`
Check: Does 3(5) + 7 = 22? → 15 + 7 = 22 ✓

'Solving' an equation just means isolating the variable — getting it alone on one side of the scale.
sections.keyInsight: Whatever you do to one side of an equation, you must do to the other. This is the entire foundation of algebra.

sections.title: Multiple Variables & Rearranging
sections.content:
Most real equations have more than one variable. You won't always be solving for a number — often you'll be rearranging to express one variable in terms of others. This is a critical skill.

Take click-through rate: `CTR = clicks / impressions`

Depending on what you know and what you need, you can rearrange it.

'I know CTR and impressions. How many clicks does that imply?'
Multiply both sides by impressions: `clicks = CTR × impressions`

Same equation. Rearranged. New question answered.

This is something you'll do constantly with statistical formulas. The formula isn't a fixed thing you look up — it's a relationship you can manipulate.
sections.keyInsight: A formula is not a fixed procedure — it's a relationship between variables that you can rearrange to answer any question you need.

sections.title: The Broken Metric
sections.content:
You're looking at an experiment dashboard. It shows a conversion rate of 4.2% for your treatment group, from a sample of 10,000 visitors. Someone asks: 'How many actual conversions was that?'

You know the relationship: `conversion_rate = conversions / visitors`

Rearrange: `conversions = conversion_rate × visitors = 0.042 × 10,000 = 420`

Now it gets interesting. Suppose your data pipeline is misbehaving and you're seeing:
- conversion_rate = 4.2%
- visitors = 10,000
- conversions = 391

Algebraic thinking immediately tells you: something is inconsistent. One of those three numbers is wrong.

You don't need a statistics degree to catch data quality problems — you need to understand equations.

sections.title: The Story of 'X'
sections.content:
Why do we use x as the default unknown? Historians have actually traced this.

The Arabic word for 'thing' or 'unknown quantity' was شيء (shay). When Arab mathematical texts were translated into Spanish in the Middle Ages, translators used xei (the Spanish x sound approximated 'sh'). This got shortened to just x.

So when you see x in a formula, you're looking at a 1,000-year-old game of telephone that started with the Arabic word for 'thing.'

René Descartes standardized the use of x, y, z for unknowns in his 1637 work La Géométrie — the same book that invented the coordinate plane. Coincidence that we plot things on x and y axes? Not at all.

problems.question: Solve for x: 5x - 3 = 17
problems.hint: Add 3 to both sides first, then divide by 5.

problems.question: You have the sample size formula: n = (Z² × p × (1-p)) / E². Rearrange to solve for E (the margin of error you can detect).
problems.hint: Multiply both sides by E², then divide both sides by n, then take the square root.
problems.explanation: Start: n = (Z² × p × (1-p)) / E². Multiply both sides by E²: n × E² = Z² × p × (1-p). Divide both sides by n: E² = Z² × p × (1-p) / n. Take the square root: E = √(Z² × p × (1-p) / n)

problems.question: A colleague says: 'Our experiment had 50,000 visitors, a 3% conversion rate, and 1,600 conversions.' Is this internally consistent?
problems.hint: Use conversions = conversion_rate × visitors and check the math.
problems.explanation: conversions = 0.03 × 50,000 = 1,500. The reported 1,600 doesn't match — one of the three numbers must be wrong. This is a data quality issue.

problems.question: If revenue = price × quantity, and revenue goes up by 10% while quantity stays the same — what happened to price? Express this algebraically.
problems.hint: Write the original and new equations. Let original price = P. New revenue = 1.1 × P × quantity.
problems.explanation: Original: R = P × Q. New: 1.1R = P_new × Q. Dividing: P_new = 1.1 × P × Q / Q = 1.1P. Price increased by 10%.

sections.title: Unit Quiz

questions.question: Which of the following is an expression (not an equation)?
questions.explanation: 3x + 7 has no equals sign — it describes a calculation but makes no claim about equality. The others all have = signs and are equations.

questions.question: Solve for x: 5x - 3 = 17. What is x?
Given CTR = clicks / impressions, rearranging to solve for clicks gives:
questions.explanation: Multiply both sides by impressions: CTR × impressions = clicks. So clicks = CTR × impressions.

questions.question: A colleague reports: 50,000 visitors, 3% conversion rate, 1,600 conversions. This data is:
options: Can't be determined without more info
questions.explanation: 0.03 × 50,000 = 1,500. The reported 1,600 doesn't match, so the data is internally inconsistent.

questions.question: Revenue = price × quantity. Revenue increases 10%, quantity stays the same. Price:
questions.explanation: If 1.1R = P_new × Q and R = P × Q, then P_new = 1.1P. Price increased by 10%.

checklist: Explain the difference between an expression and an equation in plain English
Solve a single-variable equation by applying the balance model
Rearrange a multi-variable formula to isolate any variable
Recognize when a set of numbers is internally inconsistent

units.title: Functions: What They Really Are
units.description: The most important concept in mathematics for data work. A function is the formal way of saying 'this input produces this output' — and once you truly understand functions, regression, distributions, and ML models all snap into focus.
objectives: Connect functions to real analytics scenarios

sections.title: What Is a Function?
sections.content:
A function is a rule that takes an input and produces exactly one output. Write f(x) = 2x + 3, and you have a machine: put in any number for x, get out a unique result.

In analytics, functions are everywhere: `revenue(units) = price × units` is a function. A regression model is a function. A probability distribution is a function.

The key property: one input → one output. That uniqueness is what makes functions mathematically powerful.
sections.keyInsight: A function is a deterministic machine: same input always produces the same output.

sections.title: Function Notation
sections.content:
When you see f(x), read it as 'f of x' — the value of function f at input x.

f(3) means 'evaluate f when x = 3'. If f(x) = 2x + 3, then f(3) = 2(3) + 3 = 9.

You'll see this notation constantly in statistics: P(X = k) means 'the probability that random variable X takes value k.' That's a function.
sections.keyInsight: f(x) is not f times x — it's the output of function f when given input x.

sections.title: Linear vs. Nonlinear Functions
sections.content:
A linear function has the form f(x) = mx + b. Graph it and you get a straight line. The change in output per unit change in input is constant — that constant is the slope m.

Nonlinear functions curve. f(x) = x² grows faster and faster. f(x) = log(x) grows slower and slower. f(x) = eˣ grows explosively.

Why it matters: linear regression assumes a linear relationship. When that assumption breaks down, you need nonlinear models — but understanding the linear case deeply is the prerequisite.
sections.keyInsight: In a linear function, equal changes in input produce equal changes in output. Nonlinear functions violate this — and that's often where the interesting behavior lives.

sections.title: Is This Relationship Linear?
sections.content:
You're looking at spend vs. revenue data. As you increase ad spend from $0 to $1,000, revenue grows quickly. From $1,000 to $2,000, it grows slower. From $2,000 to $3,000, barely at all.

This is diminishing returns — a nonlinear function. If you fit a linear model to this data, your predictions will be wrong in systematic, predictable ways.

Recognizing the shape of a relationship is the first step to choosing the right model.

problems.question: Is f(x) = 5x - 7 linear or nonlinear? What is its slope?
problems.hint: Look for the form f(x) = mx + b
problems.explanation: f(x) = 5x - 7 matches f(x) = mx + b with m = 5 and b = -7. It's linear with slope 5.

sections.title: Unit Quiz

questions.question: Which is a nonlinear function?
questions.explanation: x² makes f(x) = x² + 2 nonlinear — the graph is a parabola, not a line.

questions.question: What does f(3) mean?
options: the output of function f when x = 3
questions.explanation: f(3) is function notation — it means evaluate function f at input x = 3.

checklist: Evaluate a function at a given input
Identify whether a function is linear or nonlinear
Explain what function notation f(x) means

units.title: Linear Relationships & Slope
units.description: The DNA of regression. Slope tells you how much one variable changes per unit change in another — which is exactly what a regression coefficient tells you.

sections.title: Rise Over Run
sections.content:
Slope is rise over run: how much y changes per unit change in x.

Slope = (y₂ - y₁) / (x₂ - x₁)

If a line passes through (0, 2) and (3, 8): slope = (8-2)/(3-0) = 6/3 = 2.

This means: for every 1 unit increase in x, y increases by 2. That's the entire intuition behind regression coefficients.
sections.keyInsight: Slope tells you the rate of change — how many units y moves per unit of x. It's the same thing as a regression coefficient.

sections.title: The y-Intercept
sections.content:
In y = mx + b, the b is the y-intercept: the value of y when x = 0.

In regression, the intercept is the baseline — the predicted value when all predictors are zero. Sometimes this has a meaningful interpretation; sometimes it doesn't (what does 'zero height' mean for predicting weight?).

The equation y = 2x + 3 means: start at 3 (when x = 0), then move up 2 for each unit of x.
sections.keyInsight: The y-intercept is the starting value — where the line begins before x has any effect.

sections.title: Slope in Context: Regression Coefficients
sections.content:
When you run a regression and see 'coefficient = 0.42', that's a slope. It means: for each 1-unit increase in the predictor, the outcome increases by 0.42 on average.

'Users who see feature X convert at a rate 0.8 percentage points higher per additional session.' That's a slope.

This is why understanding slope is so fundamental to data work — it's the mathematical backbone of every regression result you'll ever interpret.
sections.keyInsight: A regression coefficient is a slope. Everything you know about slope applies directly to interpreting regression output.

problems.question: A line passes through points (2, 5) and (6, 13). Calculate the slope.
A regression model says: conversions = 0.04 × visitors + 12. What does the 0.04 mean?
problems.answer: For each additional visitor, conversions increase by 0.04 on average.
problems.hint: Think of 0.04 as the slope of the line.
problems.explanation: The coefficient 0.04 is the slope — for every 1 additional visitor, the model predicts 0.04 more conversions (or equivalently, ~1 conversion per 25 visitors).

sections.title: Unit Quiz

questions.question: A line passes through (1, 3) and (4, 12). What is the slope?
In y = -2x + 10, when x increases by 1, y:
questions.explanation: The slope is -2, so each 1-unit increase in x causes a 2-unit decrease in y.

questions.question: A regression coefficient of 1.5 on 'page views' means:
options: Each additional page view is associated with 1.5 more conversions
questions.explanation: A coefficient is a slope — 1.5 units increase in y per 1 unit increase in x.

checklist: Translate a regression coefficient into plain English

units.title: Exponents & Logarithms
units.description: You'll see these constantly in statistics and ML — exponential growth, log-scale charts, information theory, and model training all use them.
objectives: Connect to natural log and growth models

sections.title: Exponent Rules
sections.content:
An exponent means repeated multiplication: x³ = x × x × x.

Key rules:
- x^a × x^b = x^(a+b)  (multiply same base: add exponents)
- x^a / x^b = x^(a-b)  (divide same base: subtract exponents)
- (x^a)^b = x^(a×b)    (power of a power: multiply)
- x^0 = 1              (anything to the 0 is 1)
- x^(-a) = 1/x^a       (negative exponent = reciprocal)

These aren't arbitrary rules — they all follow from the definition of repeated multiplication.
sections.keyInsight: Exponent rules are shortcuts for repeated multiplication. Understanding them means you never have to memorize — just derive from first principles.

sections.title: What Logarithms Actually Do
sections.content:
A logarithm answers the question: 'What exponent do I need?'

log₂(8) = 3 because 2³ = 8. 'What power of 2 gives 8? Answer: 3.'

log₁₀(1000) = 3 because 10³ = 1000.

ln(x) is the natural log — log base e, where e ≈ 2.718. It's the most common log in statistics and ML.

Logarithms compress large ranges. The difference between 1 and 10 on a log scale equals the difference between 10 and 100, and between 100 and 1,000. This is why income, frequency, and many natural phenomena are better described on log scales.
sections.keyInsight: A logarithm is the inverse of an exponent. If you know one, you know the other. And log scales make exponential growth look linear — which often reveals cleaner patterns.

sections.title: Natural Log and Growth
sections.content:
The natural log ln(x) appears everywhere in statistics because the number e is the base of natural exponential growth.

If something grows at a constant rate r continuously, the growth model is: y = y₀ × e^(rt)

Taking the log of both sides: ln(y) = ln(y₀) + rt

That's a linear equation! This is why log-transforming exponentially distributed data produces normally distributed data — it linearizes the growth.

In machine learning, the log-likelihood and cross-entropy loss both use natural logs. In Bayesian statistics, log probabilities are everywhere.
sections.keyInsight: Taking the logarithm of exponential data linearizes it. This is why ln-transformation is one of the most powerful tools in a data scientist's toolkit.

problems.question: Simplify: x⁵ × x³
problems.hint: When multiplying same bases, add exponents.

sections.title: Unit Quiz

questions.question: Why are log scales useful for data with exponential growth?
options: They linearize the growth, revealing cleaner patterns
questions.explanation: Log scales compress exponential growth into a linear form — making trends and ratios easier to see.

checklist: Apply exponent rules to simplify expressions
Explain why log transformation is useful for skewed data

units.title: Summation Notation (Σ)
units.description: How statisticians write 'add a bunch of things up.' Once you can read Σ notation, statistics papers become readable.
objectives: Translate formulas between Σ notation and plain language
Recognize Σ in real statistical formulas

sections.title: Reading Σ Notation
sections.content:
Σ (sigma) means 'sum up.' The notation Σᵢ₌₁ⁿ xᵢ means: 'add up all values of x from i=1 to i=n.'

In plain language: x₁ + x₂ + x₃ + ... + xₙ

The i=1 below the Σ is the starting index. The n above is the ending index. The xᵢ is what you're summing.

Example: If you have test scores [85, 92, 78, 95, 88], then:
Σᵢ₌₁⁵ xᵢ = 85 + 92 + 78 + 95 + 88 = 438
sections.keyInsight: Σ is just a compact way to write a sum. Unpack it into 'add up x₁, x₂, ..., xₙ' and it becomes readable.

sections.title: Mean and Variance in Σ Notation
sections.content:
The sample mean formula: x̄ = (1/n) × Σᵢ₌₁ⁿ xᵢ

In English: 'add up all values, divide by n.' That's just the average.

The sample variance formula: s² = (1/(n-1)) × Σᵢ₌₁ⁿ (xᵢ - x̄)²

In English: 'for each value, subtract the mean, square the result, add them all up, divide by n-1.'

Once you can read Σ, every statistics formula becomes a readable description of what to compute — not a cryptic symbol.
sections.keyInsight: Σ notation in statistics formulas is always just an instruction to calculate something across all data points. Unpack the subscript and superscript to know what range to sum over.

problems.question: Given data: [4, 7, 2, 9, 3]. Calculate Σᵢ₌₁⁵ xᵢ (the sum).
Using the same data [4, 7, 2, 9, 3], calculate the mean x̄.
problems.hint: Mean = sum / n. You calculated the sum above.

sections.title: Unit Quiz

questions.question: The formula x̄ = (1/n) Σ xᵢ computes:
questions.explanation: This is the sample mean formula — sum all values, divide by the count.

questions.question: In the variance formula s² = (1/(n-1)) Σ(xᵢ - x̄)², the term (xᵢ - x̄) represents:
options: How far each data point is from the mean
The square root of the variance
questions.explanation: (xᵢ - x̄) is the deviation of each point from the mean — how far above or below average it is.

checklist: Read and expand Σ notation into a sum
Write the mean formula in Σ notation and in plain language
Interpret variance as the average squared deviation from the mean

expeditions.title: Descriptive Statistics & Data Intuition
expeditions.theme: What Does Your Data Actually Look Like?
expeditions.funFact: The standard deviation symbol σ (sigma) was introduced by Karl Pearson in 1894. He also invented the p-value — and famously feuded with Ronald Fisher, who then also invented the p-value slightly differently. That argument still echoes in statistics today.
expeditions.jobConnection: Every dashboard metric, every data quality check, every 'is this number weird?' question lives here.

units.title: Measures of Center — mean, median, mode (and when each one lies to you)
units.description: The three ways to find the 'middle' of data — and how to pick the right one.
objectives: Know when each measure is appropriate
Understand how outliers affect each measure

units.title: Measures of Spread — variance, standard deviation, IQR
units.description: How spread out is your data? These measures tell you.

units.title: Distributions — the shapes data makes and what they mean
units.description: Data has shapes. Recognizing those shapes tells you what's going on.

units.title: The Normal Distribution — the bell curve and why it's everywhere
units.description: The most important distribution in statistics and why it appears so often.
objectives: Explain why the normal distribution appears in nature

units.title: Percentiles, z-scores & standardization
units.description: How to compare values across different scales and distributions.
objectives: Compare apples to oranges with z-scores

units.title: Correlation vs. causation
units.description: The most important distinction in data analysis — and the most commonly confused.
objectives: Explain the difference between correlation and causation
Give real examples of misleading correlations

expeditions.title: Probability Theory
expeditions.funFact: Probability theory was essentially invented to solve gambling problems. In 1654, Blaise Pascal and Pierre de Fermat exchanged letters about a dice game dispute — and accidentally founded a field that now underpins all of science.
expeditions.jobConnection: Every A/B test, every confidence interval, every model prediction is a probability statement. This expedition is the biggest unlock for your work.

units.title: Basic probability rules — AND, OR, complements
units.description: The fundamental rules of probability.
objectives: Calculate probabilities using AND, OR rules

units.title: Conditional probability — the foundation of Bayesian thinking
units.description: How does new information change a probability?

units.title: Bayes' Theorem — how to update beliefs with evidence
units.description: The most powerful idea in probability — and perhaps all of science.

units.title: Probability distributions — Binomial, Poisson, Normal
units.description: The mathematical models for different types of random phenomena.
objectives: Connect distributions to real data types

units.title: Expected value — what 'average outcome' really means mathematically
units.description: The mathematical foundation of decision-making under uncertainty.
objectives: Connect to sample mean and long-run average

units.title: The Law of Large Numbers & Central Limit Theorem
units.description: Two of the most important theorems in all of statistics.
objectives: State the Law of Large Numbers
Understand why the normal distribution is so common

expeditions.title: Inferential Statistics & Hypothesis Testing
expeditions.funFact: Ronald Fisher introduced the 0.05 significance threshold somewhat arbitrarily in 1925 — essentially saying it was 'convenient.' The entire field has debated it ever since, and in 2019, over 800 scientists signed a paper calling for it to be abandoned.
expeditions.jobConnection: This is your experimentation work. We're going to tear apart what your testing platform does step by step.

units.title: Sampling theory — why samples represent populations
units.description: Why you can draw conclusions about millions from thousands.

units.title: Confidence intervals — what that '95%' really means
units.description: One of the most misunderstood concepts in statistics.

units.title: Hypothesis testing framework
units.description: The logical structure underlying all statistical tests.

units.title: p-values — the most misunderstood number in science
units.description: What a p-value actually means — and the four things it doesn't mean.

units.title: t-tests, z-tests, chi-square tests
units.description: The most common statistical tests and when to use each.
objectives: Choose the right test for the scenario

units.title: Statistical power, Type I & Type II errors
units.description: How to design experiments that can actually detect the effects you care about.
objectives: Define Type I and Type II errors

units.title: Multiple testing problems
units.description: Why running many tests inflates false positive rates — and what to do about it.

units.title: Bayesian vs. Frequentist inference
units.description: Two fundamentally different philosophies of statistical reasoning.
objectives: Know when each is more appropriate

expeditions.title: Regression & Predictive Modeling
expeditions.funFact: The word 'regression' comes from Francis Galton's 1886 study of heights of parents and children. He noticed children of very tall parents tended to be slightly shorter — 'regressing to the mean.' The math stuck around; the original meaning mostly didn't.
expeditions.jobConnection: Attribution models, propensity scoring, conversion prediction, causal inference in experiments — all live here.

units.title: Simple linear regression — the math of drawing the best line
units.description: The most fundamental predictive model.

units.title: Least squares — how the 'best fit' is actually calculated
units.description: The optimization principle behind regression.

units.title: Multiple regression — many variables at once
units.description: Extending regression to multiple predictors.

units.title: Regression diagnostics — how to know when your model is lying
units.description: The assumptions behind regression and how to check them.

units.title: Logistic regression — when your outcome is binary
units.description: Regression for yes/no outcomes like conversions.

units.title: Overfitting, underfitting & the bias-variance tradeoff
units.description: The fundamental tension in all of machine learning.

units.title: Introduction to regularization — Ridge, Lasso
units.description: How to prevent overfitting by penalizing complexity.

expeditions.title: Advanced Statistics for Experimentation & Data Science
expeditions.theme: The Cutting Edge of Your Field
expeditions.funFact: The word 'algebra' comes from a 9th-century Persian mathematician named al-Khwārizmī. The word 'algorithm' also comes from his name.
expeditions.jobConnection: These are the methods that separate good data science orgs from great ones — and you'll be able to evaluate and contribute to them.

units.title: Causal inference — difference-in-differences, instrumental variables
units.description: Moving beyond correlation to establish causation without randomized experiments.

units.title: Sequential testing & always-valid p-values
units.description: How to run experiments you can peek at without inflating false positive rates.
objectives: Know when to stop an experiment

units.title: Multi-armed bandits — when to stop experimenting and just ship
units.description: The explore/exploit tradeoff and its applications to experimentation.
objectives: Choose the right approach for your context

units.title: Bootstrapping & permutation tests
units.description: Simulation-based inference when analytical solutions are hard.

units.title: Dimensionality reduction concepts — PCA intuition
units.description: How to find structure in high-dimensional data.

units.title: Intro to Bayesian modeling — priors, posteriors, credible intervals
units.description: Building probabilistic models with explicit uncertainty quantification.

units.title: Effect sizes & practical vs. statistical significance
units.description: Why a statistically significant result can still be practically meaningless.
objectives: Calculate Cohen's d and other effect sizes
Design experiments around minimum detectable effects
